query: requirements for deferred defects under MEL

response.explanation:
Deferred‑defect requirements under the MEL

The following points capture the key obligations that must be met when a defect is deferred under a Minimum Equipment List (MEL). All items are taken directly from the retrieved material.

Requirement Source
Deferred defects must be identified, recorded, and deferred in accordance with approved procedures and within approved time Chunk 1
A deferred defect is a defect reported in operational service that is postponed for later rectification Chunk 2
Deferred defects must be monitored continuously Chunk 2
Deferred defects are transferred onto worksheets at the next appropriate maintenance check Chunk 3
If not rectified during that check, the defect is re‑entered on a new deferred‑defect record sheet and the original date is retained Chunk 3
Parts or components required for rectification must be ordered on a priority basis and fitted at the earliest opportunity Chunk 3
Deferred defects must be made known to the pilot/flight crew before the aircraft is flown Chunk 3
Deferred defects are listed on the current list of deferred maintenance (ML.A.305(d)(6)) Chunk 5
Deferred defects must be rectified at the next appropriate maintenance event within the limit specified in the maintenance data Chunk 5
The MEL is not controlled by the CAMO; the operating crew decides whether to accept operation with a deferred defect Chunk 4

Practical implications

  • Documentation – Every deferred defect must appear in the aircraft’s technical log or continuing airworthiness record system, and the record must be updated at each maintenance event.
  • Crew awareness – The flight crew must be informed of any deferred defect before the aircraft is used, ensuring they are aware of any operational limitations.
  • Parts readiness – Required parts should be on hand or ordered promptly so that the defect can be repaired at the earliest suitable maintenance opportunity.
  • Continuous monitoring – The CAMO defect‑control system must track all deferred and carried‑forward defects, flagging any that become repetitive or significant.

These requirements collectively ensure that deferred defects are managed safely, transparently, and in compliance with EASA regulations.
